sentences.chinese: 今魯國之鼓，當先具其備，至秋乃能舉火。
sentences.literal: Now as for Lu's smelting, one must first complete its preparations; only by autumn can fires be raised.
sentences.idiomatic: If Lu were really to fire its forges, the works would need to be readied first; the furnaces could not even be lit until autumn.

sentences.chinese: 此言與實反者非？
sentences.literal: Is this not words and facts being opposite?
sentences.idiomatic: Does that not flatly contradict what you claim?

sentences.chinese: 偃已前三奏，無詔，不惟所為不許，而直矯作威福，以從民望，干名采譽，此明聖所必加誅也。
sentences.literal: Yan had already thrice submitted memorials earlier, without edict; not only were his actions not permitted, but he directly forged authority, making awe and blessings, in order to follow the people's hopes, seeking name and harvesting reputation—this is what a clear sage must add execution to.
sentences.idiomatic: You had already memorialized three times and received no imperial reply. Not only were you never authorized to act, you forged orders, threw your weight about, pandered to popular opinion, and traded on moral prestige for personal fame—conduct that any enlightened ruler would punish with death.

sentences.chinese: 『枉尺直尋』，孟子稱其不可；
sentences.literal: 'Bending a foot to straighten eight feet'—Mencius called it unacceptable;
sentences.idiomatic: Mencius rejected the notion of bending a cubit to gain eight cubits;

sentences.chinese: 今所犯罪重，所就者小，偃自予必死而為之邪？
sentences.literal: Now the crime committed is heavy, what was accomplished is small—did you yourself grant that you would surely die and still do it?
sentences.idiomatic: Your offense is grave and your supposed public benefit slight. Did you expect execution and press ahead anyway?

sentences.chinese: 將幸誅不加，欲以采名也？」
sentences.literal: Or did you hope punishment would not be applied and wished to harvest reputation?
sentences.idiomatic: Or did you count on escaping the law and merely wanted the glory?

sentences.chinese: 偃窮詘，服罪當死。
sentences.literal: Yan was exhausted and refuted; he admitted guilt fitting death.
sentences.idiomatic: Xu Yan had no answer left; he confessed and accepted that he deserved death.

sentences.chinese: 軍奏「偃矯制顓行，非奉使體，請下御史徵偃即罪。」
sentences.literal: Jun memorialized: 'Yan forged edicts and acted on his own authority; it is not in the form of one who bears a mission; request to send down to the Imperial Clerk to summon Yan to answer for his crime.'
sentences.idiomatic: Zhong Jun reported that Xu Yan had counterfeited orders and acted arbitrarily—conduct unworthy of an imperial envoy—and asked that the case be handed to the censor so Xu Yan could be arrested and sentenced.

sentences.chinese: 奏可。
sentences.literal: The memorial was approved.
sentences.idiomatic: The throne approved the memorial.

sentences.chinese: 上善其詰，有詔示御史大夫。
sentences.literal: The emperor praised his examination; there was an edict shown to the Imperial Counselor.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor commended Zhong Jun's cross-examination and issued an edict laying out the case for the Imperial Counselor.

sentences.chinese: 初，軍從濟南當詣博士，步入關，關吏予軍繻。
sentences.literal: At first, when Jun was going from Jinan to attend the erudites, he walked into the pass; the pass clerk gave Jun a silk tally.
sentences.idiomatic: Long ago, when Zhong Jun left Jinan to enroll as a student of the imperial academy, he entered the frontier pass on foot, and the gate officer handed him the return-token of split silk.

sentences.chinese: 軍問：「以此何為？」
sentences.literal: Jun asked: 'By this, what is to be done?'
sentences.idiomatic: Zhong Jun asked, 'What is this for?'

sentences.chinese: 吏曰；
sentences.literal: The clerk said;
sentences.idiomatic: The officer replied,

sentences.chinese: 「為復傳，還當以合符。」
sentences.literal: 'It is for the return pass; when returning you must match the tally.'
sentences.idiomatic: 'It is your return chit; when you come back you must present the matching half.'

sentences.chinese: 軍曰：「大丈夫西游，終不復傳還。」
sentences.literal: Jun said: 'A great man travels west—will never use the return pass to come back.'
sentences.idiomatic: Zhong Jun said, 'A man of spirit who sets out for the west does not plan to slink home on a return pass.'

sentences.chinese: 棄繻而去。
sentences.literal: He discarded the silk and departed.
sentences.idiomatic: He threw away the token and walked on.

sentences.chinese: 軍為謁者，使行郡國，建節東出關，關吏識之，曰：「此使者乃前棄繻生也。」
sentences.literal: When Jun became Herald, sent to tour commanderies and kingdoms, bearing the scepter he went east out of the pass; the pass clerk recognized him and said: 'This envoy is that former youth who discarded the silk.'
sentences.idiomatic: Later, as court herald on an inspection tour of the commanderies, Zhong Jun rode east through the same pass with the imperial credentia staff. The gate officer recognized him and exclaimed, 'This is the young scholar who threw away his chit!'

sentences.chinese: 軍行郡國，所見便宜以聞。
sentences.literal: When Jun toured commanderies and kingdoms, whatever he saw that was advantageous he reported.
sentences.idiomatic: Wherever he went, he reported every useful measure or abuse he observed.

sentences.chinese: 還奏事，上甚說。
sentences.literal: Returning, he reported affairs; the emperor was very pleased.
sentences.idiomatic: When he returned to give account, the emperor was delighted.

sentences.chinese: 當發使使匈奴，軍自請曰：「軍無橫草之功，得列宿衛，食祿五年。
sentences.literal: When it was time to dispatch envoys to the Xiongnu, Jun volunteered, saying: 'Jun has no merit of laying grass across (the path), yet was able to be ranked among the palace guard, receiving salary five years.'
sentences.idiomatic: When envoys were to be sent to the Xiongnu, Zhong Jun volunteered: 'I have never so much as thrown a blade of grass across the battlefield for the dynasty, yet I have stood in the palace guard and drawn a stipend for five years.'

sentences.chinese: 邊境時有風塵之警，臣宜被堅執銳，當矢石，啟前行。
sentences.literal: The border from time to time has alarms of wind and dust; your servant ought to don hard armor and grasp sharp weapons, stand against arrows and stones, open the forward march.
sentences.idiomatic: Whenever the frontier flares with war, a subject ought to buckle on armor, take up arms, face arrow and stone, and lead the van.

sentences.chinese: 駑下不習金革之事，今聞將遣匈奴使者，臣願盡精厲氣，奉佐明使，畫吉凶於單于之前。
sentences.literal: Your servant is lowly and does not practice metal-and-leather affairs; now hearing that envoys will be sent to the Xiongnu, your servant wishes to exhaust essence and sharpen spirit, to assist the enlightened envoy, and paint good and ill before the Chanyu.
sentences.idiomatic: I am a poor hand at war. Now that the court will send an embassy to the Chanyu, I beg to marshal all my wit and zeal as aide to the chief envoy and to lay out before the Chanyu himself where fortune and peril lie.

sentences.chinese: 臣年少材下，孤於外官，不足以亢一方之任，竊不勝憤懣。」
sentences.literal: Your servant is young and talent is low, isolated in outer offices, not sufficient to bear one region's responsibility—privately cannot overcome frustration.'
sentences.idiomatic: I am young, my talents slight, and I have languished in minor posts without a frontier command worthy of the trust you place in me—the thought leaves me choked with frustration.

sentences.chinese: 詔問畫吉凶之狀，上奇軍對，擢為諫大夫。
sentences.literal: An edict asked about the situation of painting good and ill; the emperor marveled at Jun's reply and promoted him to Grandee Remonstrant.
sentences.idiomatic: An edict asked him to spell out what he meant by laying out good and ill fortune. The emperor was so struck by his answer that he promoted him to Grandee Remonstrant.

sentences.chinese: 南越與漢和親，乃遣軍使南越，說其王，欲令入朝，比內諸侯。
sentences.literal: Southern Yue made peace and kinship with Han; then they sent Jun as envoy to Southern Yue, persuading its king, wishing to make him enter court, comparable to inner feudal lords.
sentences.idiomatic: After Southern Yue sealed a marriage alliance with Han, the court dispatched Zhong Jun to persuade its king to come to audience and accept the status of an internal feudatory.

sentences.chinese: 軍自請：「願受長纓，必羈南越王而致之闕下。」
sentences.literal: Jun himself requested: 'I wish to receive a long cord—I will surely tie the King of Southern Yue and bring him beneath the palace gate.'
sentences.idiomatic: Zhong Jun volunteered: 'Give me a long cord and I will bind the King of Southern Yue and haul him to the palace steps.'

sentences.chinese: 軍遂往說越王，越王聽許，請舉國內屬。
sentences.literal: Jun then went to persuade the Yue king; the Yue king listened and assented, requesting to offer the whole state in internal submission.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhong Jun went south and won the king's ear; the king agreed to place his entire realm under Han.

sentences.chinese: 天子大說，賜南越大臣印綬，壹用漢法，以新改其俗，令使者留填撫之。
sentences.literal: The Son of Heaven was greatly pleased, granted Southern Yue great ministers seals and ribbons, uniformly applied Han law, in order to renew and change their customs, ordered envoys to remain to fill and pacify.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor was overjoyed. He issued Han seals and ribbons to Yue ministers, extended Han law uniformly to refashion local custom, and ordered the envoys to stay behind to stabilize the new order.

sentences.chinese: 越相呂嘉不欲內屬，發兵攻殺其王，及漢使者皆死。
sentences.literal: The Yue chancellor Lü Jia did not wish internal submission; he raised troops, attacked and killed the king, and the Han envoys all died.
sentences.idiomatic: The Yue prime minister Lü Jia refused submission, rose in arms, murdered his king, and killed the Han envoys as well.

sentences.chinese: 語在南越傳。
sentences.literal: The words are in the Biography of Southern Yue.
sentences.idiomatic: The full story is told in the biography of Southern Yue.

sentences.chinese: 軍死時年二十餘，故世謂之「終童」。
sentences.literal: When Jun died he was over twenty; therefore the world calls him 'Zhong the Boy.'
sentences.idiomatic: He was barely past twenty when he died, and posterity remembers him as 'Zhong the lad'—the boy who never grew old.

sentences.chinese: 王褒字子淵，蜀人也。
sentences.literal: Wang Bao, courtesy name Ziyuan, was a man of Shu.
sentences.idiomatic: Wang Bao, courtesy name Ziyuan, was a native of Shu.

sentences.chinese: 宣帝時修武帝故事，講論六藝群書，博盡奇異之好，徵能為楚辭九江被公，召見誦讀，益召高材劉向、張子僑、華龍、柳褒等待詔金馬門。
sentences.literal: In Emperor Xuan's time they restored Emperor Wu's precedents, lectured and discussed the six arts and collected books, broadly exhausting the fondness for strange things; they summoned one who could compose Chu Ci—Bei Gong of Jiujiang—summoned and saw him recite; further summoned talented men Liu Xiang, Zhang Ziqiao, Hua Long, Liu Bao, and others to await edict at the Golden Horse Gate.
sentences.idiomatic: Under Emperor Xuan the court revived Emperor Wu's cultural program: debate on the Six Classics and the wider corpus, a restless taste for the rare and marvelous. It summoned Bei Gong of Jiujiang, famed for his Chu-style verse, heard him recite, and then added brilliant men such as Liu Xiang, Zhang Ziqiao, Hua Long, and Liu Bao to the roster of scholars awaiting summons at the Golden Horse Gate.

sentences.chinese: 神爵、五鳳之間，天下殷當，數有嘉應。
sentences.literal: Between the Shenjue and Wufeng periods, the realm was abundant and appropriate; repeatedly there were auspicious responses.
sentences.idiomatic: Between the Shenjue and Wufeng reign titles the empire prospered, and auspicious omens arrived again and again.

sentences.chinese: 上頗作歌詩，欲興協律之事，丞相魏相奏言知音善鼓雅琴者渤海趙定、梁國龔德，皆召見待詔。
sentences.literal: The emperor somewhat composed songs and poems, wishing to raise the business of harmonizing pitch; Chancellor Wei Xiang memorialized saying those who knew tones and were good at strumming the ya zither—Zhao Ding of Bohai and Gong De of Liang—were all summoned and awaited edict.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor himself began writing lyrics and wished to revive the office for tuning pitch standards. Chancellor Wei Xiang recommended Zhao Ding of Bohai and Gong De of Liang, both masters of the classical zither, and they too were summoned to the waiting roster.

sentences.chinese: 於是益州刺史王襄欲宣風化於眾庶，聞王褒有俊材，請與相見，使褒作中和、樂職、宣布詩，選好事者令依鹿鳴之聲習而歌之。
sentences.literal: Thereupon the Governor of Yizhou, Wang Xiang, wished to spread transformation by custom among the multitude; hearing that Wang Bao had outstanding talent, he requested to meet him, had Bao compose the poems 'Central Harmony,' 'Joy in Office,' and 'Proclamation,' selected those fond of affairs and ordered them to practice and sing according to the sound of 'Deer Call.'
sentences.idiomatic: The governor of Yizhou, Wang Xiang, hoped to bring moral suasion to the common people. Learning of Wang Bao's gifts, he invited him in and commissioned the poems 'Central Harmony,' 'Joy in Duty,' and 'The Proclamation,' then picked enthusiasts to set them to the tune of the 'Deer Call' ode and teach them by singing.

sentences.chinese: 時氾鄉侯何武為僮子，選在歌中。
sentences.literal: At that time Marquis of Fanxiang He Wu was a boy; he was selected to be in the songs.
sentences.idiomatic: Among the choristers was the future Marquis of Fanxiang, He Wu, still a schoolboy.

sentences.chinese: 久之，武等學長安，歌太學下，轉而上聞。
sentences.literal: After a long time, Wu and others studied in Chang'an, sang beneath the Imperial Academy, and in turn it was heard above.
sentences.idiomatic: Years later, when He Wu and his companions were students in Chang'an, they sang the pieces below the walls of the Imperial Academy until word of them reached the throne.

sentences.chinese: 宣帝召見武等觀之，皆賜帛，謂曰：「此盛德之事，吾何足以當之！」
sentences.literal: Emperor Xuan summoned Wu and others to observe them; all were granted silk; he said to them: 'This is a matter of abundant virtue—how am I sufficient to match it!'
sentences.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan called He Wu and his fellows in to perform, rewarded each with silk, and said, 'Such music belongs to a reign of supreme virtue; how can I deserve it?'

sentences.chinese: 褒既為刺史作頌，又作其傳，益州刺史因奏褒有軼材。
sentences.literal: Bao, having composed a eulogy for the governor, also composed his biography; the Governor of Yizhou thereupon memorialized that Bao had outstanding talent.
sentences.idiomatic: Wang Bao had not only written the governor's celebratory verse but drafted his biography as well, and on that basis the governor reported Wang Bao's exceptional gifts to the capital.

sentences.chinese: 上乃徵褒。
sentences.literal: The emperor then summoned Bao.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor thereupon summoned Wang Bao to court.

sentences.chinese: 既至，詔褎為聖主得賢臣頌其意。
sentences.literal: When he had arrived, an edict ordered Bao to eulogize the meaning of 'The Enlightened Lord Obtains Worthy Ministers.'
sentences.idiomatic: Once he arrived, an edict instructed him to compose a rhapsody expounding the theme 'How a sage ruler wins worthy ministers.'

sentences.chinese: 褒對曰：
sentences.literal: Bao replied, saying:
sentences.idiomatic: Wang Bao replied:

sentences.chinese: 夫荷旃被毳者，難與道純綿之麗密；
sentences.literal: Those who bear felt and wear coarse wool—it is hard with them to speak of pure silk's beautiful density;
sentences.idiomatic: A man bundled in felt and coarse fur will not appreciate the fine weave of pure silk;

sentences.chinese: 羹黎唅糗者，不足與論太牢之滋味。
sentences.literal: Those who sup on coarse grain and hold dry rations in the mouth are not sufficient to discuss the great sacrifice's flavors.
sentences.idiomatic: one who lives on millet gruel and dry rations is no judge of the taste of a full royal banquet.

sentences.chinese: 今臣辟在西蜀，生於窮巷之中，長於蓬茨之下，無有游觀廣覽之知，顧有至愚極陋之累，不足以塞厚望，應明指。
sentences.literal: Now your servant is rustic in western Shu, born in exhausted lanes, grown up beneath tumbleweed and thatch; I have no knowledge of roaming observation and broad viewing; I only have the burden of utmost stupidity and extreme coarseness—not sufficient to fill high hopes or respond to clear directions.
sentences.idiomatic: I am a rustic from the far west, bred in a back alley and raised under a thatched roof. I have never traveled to broaden my mind; I bear instead the heaviest load of ignorance. I cannot satisfy your lofty expectations or meet the clarity of your command.

sentences.chinese: 雖然，敢不略陳愚而抒情素！
sentences.literal: Even so, how dare I not briefly state my stupidity and express plain feeling!
sentences.idiomatic: Yet I would be craven not to sketch my humble thoughts and lay bare my heart.

sentences.chinese: 記曰：共惟春秋五始之要，在乎審己正統而已。
sentences.literal: The record says: reverently consider the Spring and Autumn Annals' five beginnings' essentials—they lie in examining oneself and rectifying the lineage, that is all.
sentences.idiomatic: The classic says that the crux of the Annals' doctrine of the five beginnings is simply to know oneself and set the true succession straight.

sentences.chinese: 夫賢者，國家之器用也。
sentences.literal: As for the worthy, they are the state's tools and implements.
sentences.idiomatic: Worthy men are the instruments of state.

sentences.chinese: 所任賢，則趨舍省而功施普；
sentences.literal: If those employed are worthy, then rushing and abandoning are reduced and merit spreads broadly;
sentences.idiomatic: Employ the right men and effort is spared while their good work reaches everywhere;

sentences.chinese: 器用利，則用力少而就效眾。
sentences.literal: if tools and implements are sharp, then labor used is little yet completed effects are many.
sentences.idiomatic: give them sharp tools and little labor yields great results.

sentences.chinese: 故工人之用鈍器也，勞筋苦骨，終日矻矻。
sentences.literal: Therefore when craftsmen use blunt implements, sinews labor and bones suffer, all day toiling.
sentences.idiomatic: A craftsman with a dull blade wears himself out from dawn to dusk.

sentences.chinese: 及至巧冶鑄干將之樸，清水焠其鋒，越砥斂其咢，水斷蛟龍，陸剸犀革，忽若彗氾畫塗。
sentences.literal: When it reaches a clever smith casting Gan Jiang in the rough, clear water quenches its edge, Yue whetstone gathers its guard; in water it cuts dragons, on land it slices rhinoceros hide—suddenly as a comet sweeping, painting mud.
sentences.idiomatic: Hand that smith the rough ingot of a Gan Jiang blade, let him quench the edge in pure water and true the guard on a Yue whetstone, and the finished sword will slice dragons in the river and rhinoceros hide on land as easily as a comet streaks or a brush draws a line in wet plaster.

sentences.chinese: 如此，則使離婁督繩，公輸削墨，雖崇臺五增，延袤百丈，而不溷者，工用相得也。
sentences.literal: If it is thus, then causing Li Lou to supervise the line and Gongshu to shave the ink—even though lofty terraces increase fivefold, extending a hundred zhang, yet without confusion—it is craftsman and use suiting each other.
sentences.idiomatic: Set Li Lou to snap the plumb line and Gongshu Ban to true the ink, and terraces may rise five stories and stretch a hundred yards without the least disorder, for master and tool are perfectly matched.

sentences.chinese: 庸人之御駑馬，亦傷吻敝策而不進於行，匈喘膚汗，人極馬倦。
sentences.literal: When a mediocre man drives a nag, he also injures the mouth and wears out the whip yet does not advance in the journey; chest pants, skin sweats, man exhausted, horse weary.
sentences.idiomatic: Put a plow horse in the hands of a duffer and he will bloody its mouth and splinter his whip without gaining a mile—man panting, horse lathered, both pushed past endurance.

sentences.chinese: 及至駕齧錾，驂乘旦，王良執靶，韓哀附輿，縱馳騁騖，忽如景靡，過都越國，蹶如歷塊；
sentences.literal: When it reaches harnessing the bit-champ and file, paired horses ride at dawn, Wang Liang grasps the reins, Han Ai attends the carriage, letting loose galloping and racing, suddenly like a shadow melting, passing capitals, crossing states, stumbling as if treading clods;
sentences.idiomatic: Yoke mettlesome steeds at dawn, put Wang Liang on the reins and Han Ai beside the box, and the team flies like a dissolving shadow past cities and through whole provinces as though each stride cleared a low hillock;

sentences.chinese: 追奔電，逐遺風，周流八極，萬里壹息。
sentences.literal: pursue fleeing lightning, chase the vanishing wind, circulate the eight extremities, ten thousand li in one breath.
sentences.idiomatic: they race the lightning and hunt the tail of the storm, wheel through the eight directions, and clear ten thousand li between one breath and the next.

sentences.chinese: 何其遼哉？
sentences.literal: How vast is that!
sentences.idiomatic: How boundless that freedom!

sentences.chinese: 人馬相得也。
sentences.literal: Man and horse suit each other.
sentences.idiomatic: Horse and driver have become one.

sentences.chinese: 故服絺綌之涼者，不苦盛暑之鬱燠；
sentences.literal: Therefore those who wear fine summer silk's coolness do not suffer the sweltering heat of oppressive summer;
sentences.idiomatic: He who dons fine summer gauze does not dread the worst dog-day glare;

sentences.chinese: 襲貂狐之飕者，不憂至寒之悽愴。
sentences.literal: those who don sable and fox's chill wind do not worry about utmost cold's bitter chill.
sentences.idiomatic: wrap himself in sable and fox and he forgets the bite of deepest winter.

sentences.chinese: 何則？
sentences.literal: Why is it?
sentences.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.chinese: 有其具者易其備。
sentences.literal: Those who have its implements ease its preparations.
sentences.idiomatic: Because the right equipment makes readiness easy.

sentences.chinese: 賢人君子，亦聖主之所以易海內也。
sentences.literal: Worthy men and gentlemen are also what a sage ruler uses to ease within the seas.
sentences.idiomatic: The worthy minister is the sage emperor's tool for bringing ease to the realm.

sentences.chinese: 是以嘔喻受之，開寬裕之路，以延天下英俊也。
sentences.literal: Therefore he croons and accepts them, opens a road of breadth and surplus, in order to extend to the realm's heroes.
sentences.idiomatic: That is why he welcomes them with open encouragement, clears a broad path, and draws the empire's finest minds to his side.

sentences.chinese: 夫竭知附賢者，必建仁策；
sentences.literal: Those who exhaust wisdom and attach to the worthy surely establish benevolent plans;
sentences.idiomatic: A ruler who pours out his wit to serve the worthy will lay plans rooted in humanity;

sentences.chinese: 索人求士者，必樹伯跡。
sentences.literal: those who search for men and seek knights surely plant hegemon traces.
sentences.idiomatic: one who hunts everywhere for talent will leave the footprints of a true king.

sentences.chinese: 昔周公躬吐捉之勞，故有圉空之隆；
sentences.literal: Formerly the Duke of Zhou personally took the toil of spitting out food and grasping hair; therefore there was the height of empty prisons;
sentences.idiomatic: The Duke of Zhou interrupted every meal and shampoo to receive guests, and his reward was an age in which the jails stood empty;

sentences.chinese: 齊桓設庭燎之禮，故有匡合之功。
sentences.literal: Duke Huan of Qi established the rite of courtyard torches; therefore there was the achievement of rectifying and uniting.
sentences.idiomatic: Duke Huan of Qi lit torches in his courtyard to welcome late-night counsel, and so won the deed of binding the states in alliance.

sentences.chinese: 由此觀之，君人者勤於求賢而逸於得人。
sentences.literal: From this viewing it, one who rules men is diligent in seeking the worthy and at ease in obtaining men.
sentences.idiomatic: From this we see that the sovereign who toils to find ministers may rule at ease once they are found.

sentences.chinese: 人臣亦然。
sentences.literal: Ministers to others are also thus.
sentences.idiomatic: The same holds for those who serve him.

sentences.chinese: 昔賢者之未遭遇也，圖事揆策則君不用其謀，陳見悃誠則上不然其信，進仕不得施效，斥逐又非其愆。
sentences.literal: Formerly when worthies had not yet met encounter, in plotting affairs and measuring strategies the lord did not use their plans; in displaying and seeing sincere devotion the superior did not deem their trust true; advancing to office they could not display effect; expulsion and banishment again was not their fault.
sentences.idiomatic: Before a worthy man meets his moment, the ruler ignores every plan he lays, doubts every pledge of loyalty he offers, denies him office where he might prove himself, and casts him aside for faults that are not his own.

sentences.chinese: 是故伊尹勤於鼎俎，太公困於鼓刀，百里自鬻，甯子飯牛，離此患也。
sentences.literal: Therefore Yi Yin toiled at cauldrons and stands, Grand Duke Tai was trapped at drumming the knife, Baili sold himself, Ningzi fed oxen—leaving this misfortune.
sentences.idiomatic: Yi Yin sweated over the kitchen cauldrons, the Grand Duke Tai peddled from a butcher's block, Baili Xi sold himself into service, Ning Qi sang beside his ox—all to escape that plight.

sentences.chinese: 及其遇明君遭聖主也，運籌合上意，諫諍即見聽，進退得關其忠，任職得行其術，去卑辱奧渫而升本朝，離疏釋蹻而享膏粱，剖符錫壤而光祖考，傳之子孫，以資說士。
sentences.literal: When they meet a bright lord and encounter a sage master, planning matches the superior's intent, remonstrance is then heard, advance and retreat can connect their loyalty, holding office can enact their methods; they leave base shame and hidden filth and ascend to this court, depart coarse cloth and release straw sandals and enjoy fat and fine millet, split tallies and grant soil and brighten forefathers, transmit to sons and grandsons, thereby supplying persuading knights.
sentences.idiomatic: But let them find an enlightened sovereign, and every stratagem strikes home, every remonstrance wins a hearing, every move displays loyalty, every post lets their policy work. They rise from humble toil to stand in the central court, shed hemp shoes for robes of grain-fed silk, take investiture and fief to bring honor to the ancestors, and hand the credit down to their sons as a lesson to every persuader who waits in the cold.

sentences.chinese: 故世必有聖知之君，而後有賢明之臣。
sentences.literal: Therefore the age must have a sage-and-knowing lord, and afterward there are worthy-and-bright ministers.
sentences.idiomatic: So the age must first produce a sage-king; only then can it produce ministers of true clarity.

sentences.chinese: 故虎嘯而冽風，龍興而致雲，蟋蟀俟秋吟，蜉蝤出以陰。
sentences.literal: Therefore the tiger roars and biting wind comes; the dragon rises and brings clouds; the cricket waits for autumn to chirp; the mayfly emerges with yin.
sentences.idiomatic: The tiger's roar summons the biting gale; the dragon's ascent draws the rain clouds; the cricket waits for autumn to sing; the mayfly hatches when the yin ether gathers.

sentences.chinese: 《易》曰：「飛龍在天，利見大人。」
sentences.literal: The Changes say: 'Flying dragon in the sky—it is favorable to see the great man.'
sentences.idiomatic: The Book of Changes says, 'The dragon flies in the heavens: it furthers one to see the great man.'

sentences.chinese: 《詩》曰：「思皇多士，生此王國。」
sentences.literal: The Odes say: 'Oh, august many gentlemen, born in this king's state.'
sentences.idiomatic: The Odes sing, 'Splendid host of knights, born for this royal house.'

sentences.chinese: 故世平主聖，俊艾將自至，若堯、舜、禹、湯、文、武之君，獲稷、契、皋陶、伊尹、呂望，明明在朝，穆穆列布，聚精會神，相得益章。
sentences.literal: Therefore when the age is peaceful and the lord is sage, outstanding elders will arrive of themselves; like the lords Yao, Shun, Yu, Tang, Wen, and Wu, obtaining Ji, Xie, Gao Yao, Yi Yin, and Lü Wang—bright bright in court, solemn solemn arrayed in rows, gathering essence, meeting spirit, mutually obtaining increased splendor.
sentences.idiomatic: When the realm is at peace and the throne is wise, great men come unbidden, as when Yao, Shun, Yu, Tang, Wen, and Wu won the service of Hou Ji, Xie, Gao Yao, Yi Yin, and Lü Wang—radiant ministers ranked in solemn order, pooling their insight until ruler and subject shine the brighter together.

sentences.chinese: 雖伯牙操遞鍾，逢門子彎烏號，猶未足以喻其意也。
sentences.literal: Even if Boya grasped the Di Zhong zither, Fengmenzi bent the Wuhao bow, still they would not be sufficient to analogize its meaning.
sentences.idiomatic: Not even Boya on the Di Zhong harp or Fengmenzi drawing the Wuhao bow could capture the harmony of that meeting.

sentences.chinese: 故聖主必待賢臣而弘功業，俊士亦俟明主以顯其德。
sentences.literal: Therefore the sage lord must await worthy ministers to enlarge merit and enterprise; outstanding knights also await a bright lord to display their virtue.
sentences.idiomatic: The sage ruler waits on worthy ministers to magnify his achievement, and the brilliant minister waits on an enlightened throne to give full scope to his virtue.

sentences.chinese: 上下俱欲，驩然交欣，千載壹合，論說無疑，翼乎如鴻毛過順風，沛乎如巨魚縱大壑。
sentences.literal: Above and below both desire; joyfully they mutually rejoice; a thousand years one meeting; discussion and persuasion without doubt; winging like goose feather passing favorable wind, vast like great fish released in a great ravine.
sentences.idiomatic: When sovereign and minister share one will, they rejoice together in a match made once in a millennium, speaking as freely as a feather on a fair wind or a great fish loosed in the ocean trench.

sentences.chinese: 其得意若此，則胡禁不止，曷令不行？
sentences.literal: If obtaining intent is thus, then what barbarian prohibition does not stop, what command does not go?
sentences.idiomatic: At such a height of concord, what could resist your ban or disobey your command?

sentences.chinese: 化溢四表，橫被無窮，遐夷貢獻，萬祥畢溱。
sentences.literal: Transformation overflows the four outer zones, spreads horizontally without end; distant Yi offer tribute; myriad auspiciousness all gather thick.
sentences.idiomatic: Your transforming power spills beyond the four seas, rolls on without limit, draws tribute from the farthest tribes, and heaps every kind of blessing at your feet.

sentences.chinese: 是以聖王不遍窺望而視已明，不單頃耳而聽已聰；
sentences.literal: Therefore the sage king does not everywhere peep and gaze yet sight is already bright; does not alone incline the ear yet hearing is already keen;
sentences.idiomatic: The sage king need not crane his neck in every direction to see clearly, nor strain his ears to hear acutely;

sentences.chinese: 恩從祥風翱，德與和氣游，太平之責塞，優游之望得；
sentences.literal: grace follows auspicious wind soaring, virtue wanders with harmonious qi, the burden of great peace is filled, the hope of ease is obtained;
sentences.idiomatic: his kindness rides the auspicious breeze, his virtue mingles with the harmonious ether, the weight of perfect peace settles on his shoulders, and the dream of ruling at leisure comes true;

sentences.chinese: 遵遊自然之勢，恬淡無為之場，休徵自至，壽考無疆，雍容垂拱，永永萬年，何必偃卬詘信若彭祖，呴噓呼吸如僑、松，眇然絕俗離世哉！
sentences.literal: Following and roaming the tendency of nature, in the field of quiet plain non-action, auspicious signs arrive of themselves, long life has no border, harmonious ease with folded hands, forever ten thousand years—why must one bend and stretch and twist trust like Pengzu, exhale and inhale like Qiao and Song, subtly cut off custom and leave the world!
sentences.idiomatic: he drifts with nature's current in the stillness of wu-wei until good omens come unbidden, his years know no bound, and he may rule with folded hands for endless ages. Why should he need Pengzu's calisthenics or the breathing tricks of Wangzi Qiao and Chisongzi, or cut himself off from the human world to chase immortals?

sentences.chinese: 《詩》云「濟濟多士，文王以寧」，蓋信乎其以寧也！
sentences.literal: The Odes say, 'Many officers in array—King Wen thereby was tranquil'—indeed trustworthy that thereby he was tranquil!
sentences.idiomatic: The Odes say, 'Rank on rank of knights—that was how King Wen kept the realm at peace'—and we may trust every word.

sentences.chinese: 是時，上頗好神僊，故褒對及之。
sentences.literal: At this time the emperor somewhat favored divine immortals; therefore Bao's reply reached to it.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor was much taken with immortality cults at the time, so Wang Bao touched the theme in his answer.

sentences.chinese: 上令褒與張子僑等並待詔，數從褒等放獵，所幸宮館，輒為歌頌，第其高下，以差賜帛。
sentences.literal: The emperor ordered Bao and Zhang Ziqiao and others together to await edict; repeatedly followed Bao and others on release hunts; at favored palaces and lodges, then composed songs and eulogies, ranked their high and low, and by gradation granted silk.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor kept Wang Bao and Zhang Ziqiao on the waiting roster and often took them along on the hunt. Wherever the royal party lodged, Wang Bao would compose a celebratory piece; the emperor ranked the poems and handed out silk according to merit.

sentences.chinese: 議者多以為淫靡不急，上曰：「『不有博弈者乎，為之猶賢乎已！』
sentences.literal: Discussers mostly thought them dissolute and not urgent; the emperor said: 'Is it not said, "Are there not chess players? Doing that is still better than stopping!"'
sentences.idiomatic: Critics dismissed such verse as frivolous and beside the point of government, but the emperor replied, 'Confucius himself asked whether a game of chess was not still better than doing nothing at all.'

sentences.chinese: 辭賦大者與古詩同義，小者辯麗可喜。
sentences.literal: Ci and fu, large ones share meaning with ancient poems; small ones are eloquent and lovely and pleasing.
sentences.idiomatic: The greater rhapsodies pursue the same ends as the ancient odes; the lighter pieces charm with wit and color.

sentences.chinese: 辟如女工有綺縠，音樂有鄭衛，今世俗猶皆以此虞說耳目，辭賦比之，尚有仁義風諭，鳥獸草木多聞之觀，賢於倡優博弈遠矣。」
sentences.literal: It is like women workers having patterned silk, music having Zheng and Wei; now the vulgar custom still all by this pleases and delights ear and eye; compared to this, ci and fu still have benevolence and rightness wind instruction, birds, beasts, plants, and trees much-heard observation—better than entertainers and chess by far.'
sentences.idiomatic: Think of figured damask among weavers or the airs of Zheng and Wei among tunes: the world already delights its eyes and ears with such things. Beside them, rhapsody still carries moral allegory in birds, beasts, plants, and trees—it is infinitely superior to buffoonery or a game of weiqi.'

sentences.chinese: 頃之，擢褒為諫大夫。
sentences.literal: Before long, Bao was promoted to Grandee Remonstrant.
sentences.idiomatic: Soon afterward Wang Bao was promoted to Grandee Remonstrant.

sentences.chinese: 其後太子體不安，苦忽忽善忘，不樂。
sentences.literal: Afterward the heir's body was not at ease; he suffered hazy forgetfulness, not joyful.
sentences.idiomatic: Later the crown prince fell ill with a restless, forgetful melancholy that would not lift.

sentences.chinese: 詔使褒等皆之太子宮虞侍太子，朝夕誦讀奇文及所自造作。
sentences.literal: An edict ordered Bao and others all to go to the heir's palace to attend and serve the heir, morning and evening reciting and reading strange texts and what they themselves had composed.
sentences.idiomatic: An edict sent Wang Bao and his colleagues to the heir apparent's quarters to divert and attend him, reading aloud day and night from rare literature and from their own compositions.

sentences.chinese: 疾平復，乃歸。
sentences.literal: When the illness was pacified and restored, then they returned.
sentences.idiomatic: When the prince recovered, they were dismissed to their posts.

sentences.chinese: 太子喜褒所為甘泉及洞簫頌，令後宮貴人左右皆誦讀之。
sentences.literal: The heir was pleased with what Bao had composed, 'Sweet Springs' and 'Eulogy on the Panpipes'; he ordered the rear palace's honored persons left and right all to recite and read them.
sentences.idiomatic: The heir especially loved Wang Bao's 'Sweet Springs' rhapsody and his 'Eulogy on the Panpipes' and ordered the palace ladies in attendance to memorize them.

sentences.chinese: 後方士言益州有金馬碧雞之寶，可祭祀致也，宣帝使褒往祀焉。
sentences.literal: Afterward a fangshi said Yizhou had the treasures of the golden horse and green fowl; they could be brought by sacrifice; Emperor Xuan sent Bao to go sacrifice there.
sentences.idiomatic: Later a wizard reported that Yizhou held the spirits of the Golden Horse and Green Cock, which might be summoned by sacrifice. Emperor Xuan dispatched Wang Bao to conduct the rites.

sentences.chinese: 褒於道病死，上閔惜之。
sentences.literal: Bao died of illness on the road; the emperor pitied and regretted him.
sentences.idiomatic: Wang Bao died of illness en route, and the emperor mourned his loss.
